title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 5 (Fragment 5)
text:
After they had waited a while, both parties began to talk quietly among themselves, as if the others were not there. Tonino murmured to Paolo, “Which one is Angelica?” “I don’t know,” Paolo whispered. “Didn’t you see them at the Old Bridge then?” “I didn’t see any of them. They were all down the other—” Part of the red hanging swung aside and a lady hurried in. “I’m so sorry,” she said. “My husband has been delayed.”

Everyone in the room bent their heads and murmured “Your Grace” because this was the Duchess. But Paolo and Tonino kept their eyes on her while they bent their heads, wanting to know what she was like. She had a stiff grayish dress on, which put them in mind of a statue of a saint, and her face might almost have been part of the same statue. It was a statue-pale face, almost waxy, as if the Duchess were carved out of slightly soapy marble. But Tonino was not sure the Duchess was really like a saint. Her eyebrows were set in a strong sarcastic arch, and her mouth was tight with what looked like impatience. For a second, Tonino thought he felt that impatience—and a number of other unsaintly feelings—pouring into the room from behind the Duchess’s waxy mask like a strong rank smell.

The Duchess smiled at Old Niccolo. “Signor Niccolo Montana?” There was no scrap of impatience, only stateliness. Tonino thought to himself, I’ve been reading too many books. Rather ashamed, he watched Old Niccolo bow and introduce them all. The Duchess nodded graciously and turned to the Petrocchis. “Signor Guido Petrocchi?”

The red-bearded man bowed in a rough, brusque way. He was nothing like as courtly as Old Niccolo. “Your Grace. With me are my great-uncle Dr. Luigi Petrocchi, my elder daughter, Renata, and my younger daughter, Angelica.”

Paolo and Tonino stared at the younger girl, from her bulge of forehead to her thin white legs. So this was Angelica. She did not look capable of doing anything wrong, or interesting.

The Duchess said, “I believe you understand why—”

The red curtains were once more swept aside. A bulky excited-looking man raced in with his head down, and took the Duchess by one arm. “Lucrezia, you must come! The scenery looks a treat!”

The Duchess turned as a statue might turn, all one piece. Her eyebrows were very high and her mouth pinched. “My lord Duke!” she said freezingly.

Tonino stared at the bulky man. He was now wearing slightly shabby green velvet with big brass buttons. Otherwise, he was exactly the same as the big damp Mr. Glister who had interrupted the Punch and Judy show that time. So he had been the Duke of Caprona after all! And he was not in the least put off by the Duchess’s frigid look. “You must come and look!” he said, tugging at her arm, as excited as ever. He turned to the Montanas and the Petrocchis as if he expected them to help him pull the Duchess out of the room—and then seemed to realize that they were not courtiers. “Who are you?”

“These,” said the Duchess—her eyebrows were still higher and her voice was strong with patience—“these are the Petrocchis and the Montanas awaiting your pleasure, my lord.”

The Duke slapped a large, damp-looking hand to his shiny forehead. “Well I’m blessed! The people who make spells! I was thinking of sending for you. Have you come about this enchanter-fellow who’s got his knife into Caprona?” he asked Old Niccolo.

“My lord!” said the Duchess, her face rigid.

But the Duke broke away from her, beaming and gleaming, and dived on the Petrocchis. He shook Old Guido’s hand hugely, and then the girl Renata’s. After that, he dived around and did the same to Old Niccolo and Paolo. Paolo had to rub his hand secretly on his trousers after he let go. He was wet. “And they say the young ones are as clever as the old ones,” the Duke said happily. “Amazing families! Just the people I need for my play—my pantomime, you know. We’re putting it on here for Christmas and I could do with some special effects.”

vocabulary.definition: To wait for or expect something.
vocabulary.example: The Petrocchis and the Montanas were awaiting the Duke's pleasure.

vocabulary.definition: Smiling brightly and happily.
vocabulary.example: The Duke was beaming and gleaming, full of happiness.

vocabulary.definition: To bend your head or upper body forward as a sign of respect or greeting.
vocabulary.example: Old Niccolo bowed and introduced them all.

vocabulary.definition: Abrupt or offhand in speech or manner.
vocabulary.example: The red-bearded man bowed in a rough, brusque way.

vocabulary.definition: Taking up much space; large and heavy.
vocabulary.example: A bulky excited-looking man raced in with his head down.

vocabulary.definition: Having the ability, fitness, or quality necessary to do or achieve a specified thing.
vocabulary.example: Angelica did not look capable of doing anything wrong.

vocabulary.definition: Polite, refined, or elegant in manner.
vocabulary.example: He was nothing like as courtly as Old Niccolo.

vocabulary.definition: To move quickly and suddenly towards something or someone.
vocabulary.example: The Duke dived on the Petrocchis to shake their hands.

vocabulary.definition: Very cold in manner; stiff or formal.
vocabulary.example: The Duchess’s frigid look did not put the Duke off.

vocabulary.definition: To shine brightly, especially with reflected light.
vocabulary.example: The Duke was beaming and gleaming as he moved about.

vocabulary.definition: Simple elegance or refinement of movement or manner; courteous goodwill.
vocabulary.example: Everyone murmured 'Your Grace' when the Duchess entered.

vocabulary.definition: In a kind, polite, and generous way.
vocabulary.example: The Duchess nodded graciously and turned to the Petrocchis.

vocabulary.definition: To do something more quickly than usual or to rush somewhere.
vocabulary.example: A lady hurried in through the red hanging.

vocabulary.definition: The feeling of being annoyed or restless because you cannot do something immediately.
vocabulary.example: Her mouth was tight with what looked like impatience.

vocabulary.definition: To stop the continuous progress of an activity or process.
vocabulary.example: He had interrupted the Punch and Judy show that time.

vocabulary.definition: To speak or say something very quietly.
vocabulary.example: Tonino murmured to Paolo, “Which one is Angelica?”

vocabulary.definition: A theatrical entertainment, mainly for children, involving music, slapstick, and dancing, often performed during Christmas.
vocabulary.example: We’re putting on a pantomime here for Christmas.

vocabulary.definition: Having a strong, unpleasant smell or taste.
vocabulary.example: A strong rank smell poured into the room from behind the Duchess’s waxy mask.

vocabulary.definition: Not able to be bent or changed; stiff and unyielding.
vocabulary.example: The Duchess's face was rigid when addressed by the Duke.

vocabulary.definition: Using irony to mock or convey contempt.
vocabulary.example: Her eyebrows were set in a strong sarcastic arch.

vocabulary.definition: In poor condition through long use or lack of care.
vocabulary.example: He was now wearing slightly shabby green velvet.

vocabulary.definition: Visual or audio illusions used in theatre or film productions to create imagined events.
vocabulary.example: We could do with some special effects for the pantomime.

vocabulary.definition: Having a dignified, unhurried, and grand manner; majestic in appearance.
vocabulary.example: There was no scrap of impatience, only stateliness.

vocabulary.definition: A carved or cast figure of a person or animal, especially one that is life-size or larger.
vocabulary.example: She had a stiff grayish dress on, which put them in mind of a statue of a saint.

vocabulary.definition: To pull something suddenly or strongly.
vocabulary.example: He tugged at her arm, urging her to come.
